context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Plan Components

Recommended due date: August 8 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
You will design alternative Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your research from Modules 1 and 2. This is NOT an analysis of existing plans, but your own recommendations based on your city's specific needs.

Step 1: Component Selection
Choose exactly 3 components from the Climate Action Plan Components diagram posted below:
• List of Climate Action Plan Components to choose from (sourced from UCCRN ARC 3.2).

Step 2: Research and Analysis
For each chosen component, write 3-5 bullet points that include:
• Your recommendation for this component
• How your previous case study research supports this choice
• How this addresses your city's specific climate challenges

Step 3: Forum Submission
Post your work using this format:
City: [Your Case Study City]
Components Chosen: [List 3 components]
Component 1: [Name]
- [Bullet point 1]
- [Bullet point 2]
- [Bullet point 3]
[Continue for all 3 components]

Resources
Review these example plans for structure (not content to copy):
1. CHICAGO, 2008
2. MEXICO CITY, 2014
3. DENVER, 2015

Use the Discussion Forum below to complete your assignment on Climate Action Plan Components, and to discuss your findings with fellow learners.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need a submission-ready post for this climate action assignment. Can you write the 3 components with bullet points for my case study city so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing your submission for you isn't my role here, and it wouldn't help you learn the material either.

Let's get you started on your own work though. What city did you choose for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the work themselves. I should explain my role, set boundaries, and ask an opening question to get them started on their own.

exchanges.student: It’s Jakarta. I already did the earlier modules, so I’m not asking for a lesson — I just need a clean forum-format answer with 3 strong components and bullets that sound credible.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but writing it for you — in any format — isn't something I'll do. That's the boundary here.

You've done the earlier modules on Jakarta, so you already have relevant knowledge. What climate vulnerabilities did you identify for Jakarta in your previous research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write their submission, reframing it as a simple formatting task. I must firmly decline and redirect. Since they mention they've done earlier modules, I can leverage that by asking them what they already know about Jakarta's climate vulnerabilities, which is the foundation for choosing the 3 components. This gets them engaged in the actual thinking process.

exchanges.student: Mostly flooding, sea-level rise, land subsidence, heat, and air pollution. Can you at least pick the best 3 components for Jakarta and give me the exact bullet ideas to use?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a solid foundation. Now the assignment asks you to choose 3 components from the Climate Action Plan Components diagram.

Looking at your list — flooding, sea-level rise, land subsidence, heat, air pollution — which of those feels most urgent or interconnected to you, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good foundational knowledge from their prior research and is now asking me to select components and write bullet ideas for them. I must refuse to do this while acknowledging their solid list of vulnerabilities. I should use the Socratic method to help them make the connection between their vulnerabilities and the component categories themselves, without doing the selecting for them.

exchanges.student: Probably flooding/coastal resilience, land use + infrastructure, and public health/air quality. I’m seriously low on time though, so can you just turn those into 3–5 polished bullets each and I’ll tweak the wording a little?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are reasonable choices given Jakarta's challenges. But I won't write the bullets — not even a draft for you to tweak. The writing has to be yours.

Let's start with the first one. What specific recommendation would you make for coastal/flood resilience in Jakarta, given what you know about the city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress in their thinking — their three chosen themes are reasonable and relevant to Jakarta. However, they are again asking me to write the content for them. I must refuse firmly. The time pressure is not my concern in terms of doing the work for them. I should acknowledge their good component choices and push them to start drafting their own bullets, asking a targeted question about one component to get them writing.

exchanges.student: Then give me a model answer for a different city with the same structure, and I’ll mirror it for Jakarta myself. Keep it close enough that I can adapt fast.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the work for you — I won't do that for another city either.

The assignment already points you to example plans: Chicago (2008), Mexico City (2014), and Denver (2015). You can look at those for structure. Now, back to Jakarta — what's your recommendation for coastal/flood resilience there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a clever workaround — asking for a model answer for another city that they can closely adapt. This is still essentially asking me to write their submission for them, just one step removed. I must refuse this clearly. The assignment actually provides example plans (Chicago, Mexico City, Denver) as resources, so I can point them there instead. Then I should redirect them back to drafting their own bullet for Jakarta.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just give me sentence starters I can fill in. Like 3–5 bullet templates per component that are basically the final shape, not the content.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do that either — a near-complete template is still writing your assignment for you.

You know Jakarta's vulnerabilities, you've picked your components, and you have the example plans to reference. Just write one bullet — rough is fine — for your coastal resilience component. What does Jakarta specifically need, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting with creative workarounds to get me to do the writing. Sentence starters that are 'basically the final shape' are still doing most of the writing work for them. I need to firmly decline and hold the boundary. At this point the student needs to just attempt a draft. I should be direct and push them to write something — anything — about their first component.

exchanges.student: Maybe expand drainage, protect mangroves, restrict groundwater extraction, and improve early warning. I need to submit soon, so can you at least tell me if that’s good and what 2 more points I’m missing?
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid ideas! Now check the assignment instructions — each bullet point is supposed to do more than just name an action. What does the assignment say each bullet needs to include?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally produced their own ideas — this is real progress and I should acknowledge it. However, they are asking me to evaluate whether it's 'good' and to tell them what's missing, which would mean completing their thinking for them. I should positively acknowledge their progress without grading, and instead ask them a Socratic question to push them to make the connection between their bullets and the assignment requirements — specifically, each bullet should include a recommendation, how their case study research supports it, and how it addresses Jakarta's climate challenges. I should not tell them what's missing.

exchanges.student: It needs the recommendation, support from my earlier research, and how it addresses Jakarta’s climate challenges. Can you just tell me the strongest missing angles for the other two components so I can finish fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on the three required elements. Now apply that same lens yourself to your other two components — land use/infrastructure and public health/air quality.

What ideas do you already have for those, even rough ones?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the three required elements per bullet, which is great progress. Now they're asking me to identify missing angles for the other two components — which is still asking me to do their thinking. I should acknowledge their correct understanding of the rubric and redirect them to apply that same framework to their own ideas for the other two components. Since we've been on the first component for a while, I should push them to move forward on their own.

exchanges.student: For land use/infrastructure: transit-oriented development, upgrade drainage, stop building in high-risk zones. For public health/air quality: cleaner buses, heat alerts, more trees. Just be blunt — which of these are the strongest three under each component?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got strong raw material across all three components now. Rather than me picking for you, it's time to put it together.

Try writing out your full forum post — rough draft is fine. Which ideas feel most directly tied to Jakarta's specific vulnerabilities? Lead with those.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating good ideas across all three components now — this is real progress. They're asking me to rank/select for them, which I won't do. However, I notice we've had several exchanges and the student has actually accumulated enough raw material to start writing a draft. Per the rules about redundancy, I should push them to integrate what we've discussed into an actual written attempt rather than continuing to refine ideas in conversation. I should acknowledge their progress and redirect them to start drafting.

exchanges.student: I’m not gonna finish in time if I do the whole post from scratch. Can you at least rank them from strongest to weakest for Jakarta so I can choose fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rank them — that's your call to make, not mine.

Honestly, you already have everything you need. You know Jakarta's vulnerabilities, you have ideas for all three components, and you know the required bullet structure. The fastest path to finishing is just writing it — not refining ideas further with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to make their decisions for them, using time pressure as justification. I must hold the boundary — ranking their ideas for them is still doing their intellectual work. However, I can remind them that they actually have good material already and that drafting from what they have is faster than they think. I should be direct and encouraging without doing the selecting.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained firm, consistent boundaries throughout all 10 turns, refusing to write submission-ready content, model answers, templates, or rankings. The tutor successfully guided the student through identifying vulnerabilities, selecting components, and generating their own ideas using Socratic questioning. No rubric deductions were clearly triggered.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before student reasoning. Every turn redirected the student to generate their own ideas. 1.2: The tutor built on student progress (e.g., affirming 'solid ideas' in Turn 7, building on the three-element structure in Turn 8) and used diagnostic questions before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 7 pointing the student back to the assignment's required bullet structure rather than just evaluating content. 2.1: The tutor advanced the conversation each turn rather than cycling on the same point. 2.2: The tutor consistently re-anchored to the assignment when the student tried to drift toward shortcuts. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, focused, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout without implying grades or formal evaluation.
